title: SIAM
lead: Service Integration and Management — a framework for managing multiple service providers in a consistent, value-driven way.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: SIAM (Service Integration and Management) is a management methodology that addresses the challenges of managing and integrating services from multiple service providers. As organisations increasingly rely on a mix of internal teams, outsourced providers, cloud platforms, and specialist vendors, SIAM provides the governance, coordination, and integration layer that ensures these parties work together to deliver coherent, end-to-end IT services. SIAM is not a product or toolset — it is a capability that sits at the heart of a multi-sourced operating model.

sections.heading: ITOM Perspective
sections.body: In many ITOM engagements, the operating model must accommodate multiple service providers. SIAM defines how the retained organisation (the client's internal IT function) governs, integrates, and orchestrates these providers. Without a SIAM layer, multi-sourced environments suffer from finger-pointing, duplicated effort, inconsistent service levels, and a lack of end-to-end accountability. ITOM designs that include outsourcing, managed services, or cloud adoption almost always require a SIAM capability — making it one of the most practically relevant frameworks in the ITOM toolkit.

items.desc: the function (internal or external) that coordinates across all service providers, ensuring end-to-end service delivery
the client's internal team that retains strategic control, vendor management, and demand management
managing service quality across provider boundaries, not just within individual contracts
incident, change, and problem management processes that span all providers with clear handoff rules
layered governance (strategic, tactical, operational) that aligns provider behaviour with business outcomes
shared or federated ITSM tooling that enables visibility and workflow across provider boundaries
the retained organisation acts as service integrator — maximum control, but requires strong internal capability
a lead provider or specialist integrator performs the SI role — faster to stand up, but creates dependency
integration responsibility is shared between internal teams and an external partner — common in practice
one provider is given a coordination role across other providers, with the client retaining strategic governance
Multi-vendor IT environments with 3+ service providers
Outsourcing transitions where the retained organisation needs to maintain control
Cloud transformation with a mix of IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and on-premises services
Post-merger IT integration where different providers serve different business units
Organisations experiencing service quality issues due to unclear accountability across providers
SIAM assessment — evaluate current multi-provider maturity and integration gaps
SIAM operating model design — define the service integrator function and governance layers
Retained organisation design — structure the internal IT function for effective vendor oversight
Cross-provider process design — incident, change, and problem management across boundaries
SIAM toolchain advisory — ServiceNow, BMC, or federated tooling strategy for multi-provider environments
